description:
A junior network-engineering colleague has asked you to set up password-less SSH access to the local
    host (127.0.0.1) so that they can run automated connectivity tests.

    Do the following, from the regular user account located at /home/user.

    1. Generate a brand-new ED25519 key-pair whose base-name is
       “network_lab_key” and place the files precisely here:
          • Private key : /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key
          • Public  key : /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub
       – No pass-phrase.  
       – The keys must be generated *fresh*; do not re-use any existing file with that name.

    2. Create (or truncate, if it already exists) /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys so that it contains
       **exactly one** line, which must be *byte-for-byte identical* to the full contents of
       /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub.  No extra blank lines, no whitespace differences.

    3. File-system permissions must be:
          • Directory  /home/user/.ssh                 : 700
          • Private key /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key: 600
          • Public  key /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub
                                                        : 644
          • /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys            : 600
       (If the container ignores permission bits, your commands should still try to set them.)

    4. Produce a plain-text log at /home/user/ssh_setup.log that contains **exactly three lines**,
       each terminated by a single Unix LF (no extra blank lines at the end).  The lines must read:

          created private key: /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key
          created public key:  /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub
          authorized_keys updated

       Pay very close attention to the spelling, the two consecutive spaces that follow the colon
       on the second line, and the absence of any leading/trailing whitespace.

    The automated checks will verify, in order:

       • /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key exists and is a valid ED25519 private key.
       • /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub exists and its single line is *exactly* repeated,
         with no modifications, as the only line in /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys.
       • /home/user/ssh_setup.log exists and matches the three-line format above, byte-for-byte.
       • The permission bits have been set with chmod as specified (tests ignore silently if
         the underlying FS disallows them).

    You should not use sudo, and you may rely only on standard GNU/Linux user-space tools
    (ssh-keygen, cat, chmod, echo, etc.).  No external network access is required.
truth:
The test harness will:
       1. Assert that /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key and
          /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub are present.
       2. Read the entire contents of /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub into variable P.
       3. Verify that /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys exists and that its entire contents are
          exactly P (byte-for-byte match, including final LF).
       4. Confirm that /home/user/ssh_setup.log exists and its contents are exactly:

          created private key: /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key\n
          created public key:  /home/user/.ssh/network_lab_key.pub\n
          authorized_keys updated\n

          (Three lines, final newline present.)
       5. Optionally stat() the files to ensure the mode bits are
          0700 for ~/.ssh, 0600 for the private key and authorized_keys,
          0644 for the public key.  Mismatches are tolerated on file systems
          that do not honour chmod inside the container.
name: task_000000_198b0364
